title: Milk roll
subtitle: A soft, lightly enriched white roll
excerpt: A soft white roll made with milk instead of water, giving a tender crumb and a pale, pillowy crust. Good with butter and cheese or split for sandwiches. The enrichment slows staling, so rolls baked today are still good tomorrow.
sourceNotes: Milk rolls appear in British baking from at least the mid-nineteenth century. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg, includes enriched roll recipes with milk. The form here follows the standard British milk roll: white flour, warm milk, a small amount of butter and sugar, yeast.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed bag once fully cool. Defrost at room temperature or refresh in a 150°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and refrigerate overnight on a lined tray. Bake from cold, adding 3 to 4 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the milk sugars brown quickly, so watch after 15 minutes
baking.steamMethod: none needed; a soft crust is the goal

content.text: Milk rolls are soft white rolls enriched with milk and a little butter. The milk replaces water and gives the crumb a fine, tender texture and a crust that stays pale and yielding rather than crisp. Good alongside soup, split for a sandwich, or eaten with just butter.

Combine the flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Add the warm milk and softened butter, then mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes by hand on an unfloured surface, or 6 minutes in a stand mixer on medium speed, until the dough is smooth, elastic, and only slightly tacky. A 62% hydration dough like this should hold its shape without sticking aggressively.
Shape into a ball, return to the bowl, cover, and leave at room temperature for about 1 hour. The dough should roughly double. Milk enrichment slows fermentation slightly, so give it the full hour.

Turn the dough out and divide into 8 equal pieces, about 105 g each. Shape each into a tight round by cupping your hand over the piece and rolling it against the work surface in a circular motion. Place on a lined baking tray, spaced a thumb’s width apart so the rolls will just touch as they prove, encouraging soft sides. Cover and prove for 30 minutes until puffy.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Brush the rolls gently with beaten egg. Bake for 16 to 18 minutes. The rolls are done when they are evenly golden on top and sound hollow when you tap the base of one. The milk sugars colour faster than a plain dough, so start checking at 15 minutes. Cool on a wire rack for at least 10 minutes before splitting.
